summary: Use the SQLite connection-open path as the schema lifecycle boundary. Adding a small `schema` option to `createSqliteRuntime` kept DB-004 local and avoided compiler-surface churn.
ergonomics: `createSqliteRuntime` already had a clean open-time seam, so migration and compatibility handling fit without touching the checker or emitter.
The runtime integration tests in `test/Main.hs` are effective for app-surface behavior, but the JSON snapshot assertions are brittle and require careful ordering.
follow_ups: Export a tiny shared helper for reading and writing `PRAGMA user_version` if more hosts need schema hooks.
Consider promoting schema-version expectations into generated binding metadata once SQLite apps need compiler-visible upgrade planning rather than host-only callbacks.
warnings: Schema hooks run before `onOpen`; if migration or compatibility fails, the runtime closes the database and removes the connection from the live map immediately.
Readonly connections never attempt migrations, so incompatibility handling depends entirely on the provided compatibility hook when versions differ.
tests_run: `cabal test --test-options='--pattern=sqlite runtime'` (failed in the raw shell because `ghc` was not on PATH)
residual_risks: The new schema lifecycle is host-runtime-only; compiler contracts do not yet describe migration plans or expected schema versions.
Compatibility decisions are callback-defined, so callers can still accept stale schemas if their hook is too permissive.
Readonly opens intentionally skip migrations; apps that want softer behavior than strict version matching must provide a compatibility hook.
